answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété* (Volumes I and II) are the primary works in the corpus dedicated to property rights.** These Enlightenment-era treatises systematically analyze property as a fundamental right, its origins, limits imposed by law, and applications to land, literary works, communal properties, and more.[^1][^2] Volume I covers topics like public domain dependencies, navigable rivers, mines, and servitudes on private property.[^3] Volume II extends to literary property, accession rights, and critiques of legal restrictions on disposition.[^4]

Ancient Indian *dharmaśāstra* texts like *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* and *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* address property rights extensively in legal contexts, including sales, debts, pledges, inheritance, deposits, partitions, and punishments for disputes over ownership—though more as prescriptive law than philosophical theory.[^5][^6] These single-chapter compilations detail procedures for immovable/movable property, creditor claims, and protections against seizure.[^7]

Comte's related *Traité de législation* series (Vols. I–IV) likely touch on property in political theory but are not centered on it.[^8] No other works match as directly; the corpus lacks classical treatments like Locke's.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, work wiki overview (distinctive focus on property rights, public domain, servitudes). [Work wiki](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/wiki/_work.card.md).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, work wiki overview (focus on enjoyment/disposition rights, literary property). [Work wiki](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/wiki/_work.card.md).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Ch. 15: "The woods of private individuals are therefore subject, with respect to property rights, to two transitory exceptions..." [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/15-des-lois-destinees-a-prevenir-le-deboisement-des-m/translation/#p-e5607c].
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, Ch. 33: "...have engaged in philosophical discussions on property rights?" [/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/33-des-lois-relatives-a-la-propriete-des-compositions/translation/#p-f14bea].
[^5]: Unknown, *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1: rules on obstructing rites to seize property. [/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-15cc9c].
[^6]: Unknown, *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Ch. 1: "When property is sold or taken for the purpose of a debt..." [/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-6805b9].
[^7]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* and *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* wiki overviews (legal disputes, ownership transfers). [Bṛhaspati wiki](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/wiki/_work.card.md); [Āṅgirasa wiki](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/wiki/_work.card.md).
[^8]: Structural relations from *Traité de la propriété* Vols. I/II (same-author links to *Traité de législation*).
